metadata.title: Why No Algorithm Can Be Fair Three Ways at Once
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism candidate 03. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/07-fairness-metrics-choosing-a-definition-and-defending-it.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty blue = group A, terracotta = group B (group identity, not good/bad), gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty blue #5B7B9C = group A; terracotta #D35F43 = group B — GROUP IDENTITY colors, morally neutral in this film; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Numbers verified by computation via Chouldechova's relation v/(1-v) = p/(1-p) * t/f (FACTCHECK.md). Base rates 0.6/0.3. Equalized-odds state: TPR .70/.70, FPR .15/.15, PPV .88/.67 (chapter table CONSISTENT). Calibration state: TPR .83/.50, FPR .20/.04, PPV .86/.86 — CORRECTED from the chapter's table, whose Group-B row (FPR .10 with PPV .83) fails the relation (gives .68); chapter erratum filed. Theorem provenance: Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan and Chouldechova, both 2016, independent. Card exclusions: no algebra derivation on screen, no COMPAS case, no individual/counterfactual fairness, no GE index, no debiasing toolkit.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Three definitions of fairness. Each one obviously reasonable. And a theorem — proved twice in the same year — says your model cannot satisfy all three at once.

card.copy: Why no algorithm can be fair three ways at once

beats.narration_text: You've built a model that answers yes or no. Loans, hiring, risk. It's applied to two groups of people, and you want it to be fair.
beats.scene_description: a printed decision form pinned to newsprint — a plain application sheet with prominent YES / NO checkboxes, clinical and anonymous — slow push-in on the checkboxes
beats.image_prompt: printed application decision form with two large checkboxes labeled yes and no, anonymous and clinical, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage, no names or faces

beats.narration_text: Attempt one: both groups should hear 'yes' at the same rate. Equal treatment in the decisions themselves.

production_viz.label: 1 · equal yes-rates
production_viz.mechanic: two rows of ten outline squares (blue group, terracotta group); the same number fill in each row — equal share of yeses, drawn countable
production_viz.note: demographic parity without the term; 4 of 10 fill in each row

beats.narration_text: Attempt two: the mistakes should fall evenly. The same false-alarm rate, the same miss rate, in both groups.
beats.scene_description: the two group rows persist; two chips land — 'false alarms: equal' and 'misses: equal' — stamped across both rows

production_viz.label: 2 · equal error rates
production_viz.mechanic: the group rows persist smaller; two slate chips land spanning both groups: 'false alarms — equal' / 'misses — equal'

beats.narration_text: Attempt three: the score should be honest for everyone. When the model says seventy percent, it means seventy percent — in both groups.

production_viz.label: 3 · an honest score
production_viz.mechanic: a chip reading 'the model says: 70%' floats above the two rows of ten; exactly 7 fill in the blue row and 7 in the terracotta row — the score means the same thing, drawn countable
production_viz.note: calibration parity without the term; 7 of 10 in each row

beats.narration_text: All three sound like fairness. In twenty sixteen, two teams independently proved the same theorem: when the groups' base rates differ, no model can satisfy all three.

document.quote: You can have any two. Not three.

beats.scene_description: the theorem's verdict set large in charcoal serif; gold highlighter sweeps 'two' as it is spoken; the attribution names both 2016 proofs
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] the theorem card — any two, not three

beats.narration_text: Here's the machine. The groups arrive with different base rates — in one, sixty percent repay; in the other, thirty. One threshold turns scores into yeses, and with an honest score, the error rates already disagree.

production_viz.label: two groups, one threshold, three dials
production_viz.mechanic: two score-distribution panels (blue A massed right, terracotta B massed left) with a vertical threshold line each; a metric table on the right — rows: false alarms, misses caught (TPR), 'a yes is right' (PPV) — starts in the HONEST-SCORE state: TPR .83/.50, FPR .20/.04, PPV .86/.86
production_viz.note: calibration state numbers CORRECTED via Chouldechova relation (chapter's B-row was inconsistent — see FACTCHECK)

beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] the machine — distributions, threshold, dial table

beats.narration_text: So slide the threshold until the mistakes fall evenly. Done — false alarms equal, misses equal. But now a 'yes' is right far more often in one group than the other.

production_viz.label: fix the errors — break the score
production_viz.mechanic: group B's threshold line slides right (.5 → .6); the table Transforms to the equalized-odds state: TPR .70/.70, FPR .15/.15 (gold EQUAL chips land on those rows) — and PPV splits to .88 vs .67
production_viz.note: chapter's equalized-odds table, verified consistent: .875 and .667 by the relation

beats.new_visual_element: [TRANSFORM] errors lock equal; the score's meaning splits

beats.narration_text: Slide it back to make the score honest again — and the error rates tear apart instead. Every fix breaks the other dial. That is the theorem, drawn.
beats.scene_description: the threshold slides back; the table returns to the honest-score state; the editor's pen rings the now-split error rows (.83/.50 and .20/.04) — the film's single ring, timed to 'tear'

production_viz.mechanic: B's threshold returns (.6 → .5); table Transforms back: PPV .86/.86, TPR .83/.50, FPR .20/.04; HandRing lands around the two error rows
production_viz.note: one ring only — the film's single ring

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the ring on the torn-apart error rates

beats.narration_text: The only exits are a perfect model or identical base rates. Short of those, the three dials are welded to one lever. Something has to give.

production_viz.label: any two — never three
production_viz.mechanic: a triangle of three serif nodes — equal yeses · equal errors · honest score; two nodes take gold check chips, the edge to the third snaps (dashed, terracotta X); the checks then rotate to a different pair and a different edge snaps — whichever two you pick, the third breaks
production_viz.note: the chapter's fig 7.1 in motion; no algebra

beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] the triangle — pick two, the third snaps

beats.narration_text: So when two audits of one algorithm reach opposite verdicts, both can have done the math right. The disagreement isn't arithmetic — it's about which fairness should win.
beats.scene_description: the values question set large; gold sweeps 'win' — the generic two-audits framing carries the COMPAS lesson without the case (card exclusion)

beats.narration_text: The theorem doesn't excuse you from fairness. It forbids you from claiming all of it — and it forces you to defend the piece you chose.

card.copy: Fairness is a choice. The theorem just makes it honest.
card.sub: from Computational Skepticism for AI — chapter 7
